FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 11,878 9-10-year-olds, 21 recruitment sites, NIMH Data Archive; data release 3.0

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal screen usage data, psychometric validation of SMAQ/VGAQ, factor structures with invariance testing across sex and race/ethnicity

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, published 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Pure psychometric/validation paper. SMAQ and VGAQ are measured and their factor structures/measurement properties examined, but no external individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being) is paired with these SM/gaming measures as IV or DV in any analysis. Demographic differences (sex, race/ethnicity) reported but other side is demographic-only, not individual-trait-level.

notes: FT-IC2 fails because paper does not pair SM/gaming variables with an individual-trait-level outcome or predictor; demographic-only comparisons do not satisfy the criterion.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline (2.0.1 release, 2019), 9-10-year-olds, 21 sites across US, fMRI and behavioral data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional case-control study comparing video gamers (VGs) and non-video gamers (NVGs) on cognitive performance measured by fMRI and behavioral tasks

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open, published October 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Video gaming (phone/device-specific, self-reported hours per week) is IV; cognitive performance (individual-trait behavioral outcome) measured by fMRI tasks (stop signal task, n-back) is DV. Qualifies: gaming is isolated modality, paired with within-person cognitive/neural trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Stop signal task: reaction time (SST) mean differences (287.3 vs 300.1 ms, p=0.02), correct go RT (514 vs 552 ms, p=0.002); n-back task: D' means (2.33 vs 2.18, p<0.01), reaction times faster in VGs; fMRI BOLD activation reported in precuneus, occipital cortex, cingulate, frontal gyri with quantified contrasts and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported: mean reaction times with SMD, t-test p-values, fMRI BOLD signal contrasts, effect sizes (SMD 0.04-0.5); all qualify as numeric inferential results for the gaming-cognition relationship

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 does not apply to cross-sectional studies regardless of which side SM/gaming is on
notes: Clear qualitative match: video gaming isolated and paired with cognitive outcome variables (fMRI activation, behavioral task performance). Meets FT-IC1 through FT-IC4; FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, >3,000 young adolescents (11-14 years), multisite (21 sites, 17 states), data from ABCD 3.0 release and COVID-19 rapid response surveys (May-August 2020)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; machine learning classification models (XGBoost, logistic regression) trained to predict pandemic-related mental health outcomes from prepandemic and pandemic psychosocial/lifestyle predictors

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health, published 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time appears as a generic predictor variable ('more screen time' identified as risk factor for anxiety, depression) within a multi-variable machine learning model predicting mental health outcomes. However, screen time is not isolated as smartphone or social media use; the paper bundles multiple screen modalities including pandemic-related news watching, watching videos, playing single-player video games, and browsing internet as undifferentiated 'screen time' predictors. No per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM from other screen activities. Composite screen time without phone/SM isolation does not qualify under FT-IC2.

notes: FT-IC2 fails because screen time is aggregated/composite without breakdown isolating smartphone or social media from other screen modalities (TV watching, video games, internet browsing treated as undifferentiated screen predictors).
